nativeRange.habitat: Clear coastal reefs and protected outer reef habitats with rich invertebrate growth, typically at depths of 20–40 m

diet.notes: Has been documented accepting a varied diet including shaved shrimp, squid, scallop, mysis shrimp, freeze-dried krill soaked in vitamin supplement, and frozen marine algae. Initial acceptance of prepared foods can prove challenging; embedding food in coral rubble has been reported to help encourage consumption. Generally advised to feed at least three times daily.

avoidWith: other filefish of the same genus

summary: The Colored Filefish (Pervagor melanocephalus) is a reef-associated filefish with a striking blackish-brown head and vibrant orange body. Generally considered peaceful, it tends to be shy and is commonly kept singly or in pairs. Initial feeding can require some coaxing.
careNotes: Also sold as the Blackheaded Filefish or Redtail Filefish. Displays a distinctive coloration: blackish-brown or bluish-black head and anterior body, with the remaining body orange-red; orange caudal fin and yellow soft dorsal and anal fins. Specimens from Hawaii have been reported to exhibit more vibrant coloration than those from the Indian Ocean. Generally considered reef-safe, though individual variation exists and isolated cases of invertebrate nipping have been reported. Has been known to show aggression toward other filefish of the same genus (Pervagor); otherwise tends to be peaceful with dissimilar tankmates. Described as secretive by nature and often encountered as adult pairs in the wild, typically at deeper reef habitats (20–40 m). Initial feeding may prove challenging — embedding food in live rock or coral rubble can help encourage consumption. Generally advised to feed a minimum of three times daily with varied foods. Note on size: aquarium trade sources (LiveAquaria) cite a typical adult size of approximately 4 inches (10 cm), while scientific records (FishBase) document a maximum total length of 16 cm — the larger value reflects maximum field-recorded specimens; typical captive adults are generally smaller.
